interactions.summary: Jiang explains the mechanisms of secret societies: hierarchy preserves secrecy, shared transgression creates trust, ritual sacrifice is the most basic transgression, and eschatology gives coordination by telling members they serve a higher world-conquering purpose.

He turns empire into a struggle among secret societies: factions stop caring about the empire, become insular, corrupt, and divided, and invite poor borderland mercenaries into their struggles. Those mercenaries gain wealth, technology, and access, then take over.

He introduces the classroom World Game: teams become countries with unequal envelopes of resources, sell commodity shapes to the World Bank, and may trade, beg, work, or borrow across unequal starting positions.

speaker_notes.note: Continuous Jiang lecture. The phrase transcribed as 'complementarization' likely points to compartmentalization within hierarchy.

claims.claim: Secret societies solve secrecy through hierarchy, trust through shared transgression, and coordination through eschatological myth.
In the empire stage, factions care less about the empire than about their own team emerging on top, which makes the center insular, corrupt, and divided.
claims.temporal_scope: Imperial faction model in this lecture.

claims.claim: Borderland tribes gain their path to conquest because imperial factions invite them in as mercenaries, giving them wealth, technology, weapons, and political access.
claims.temporal_scope: General conquest model with Qing and Macedonian examples.

claims.claim: To forecast conquest, Jiang says to compare which areas are energetic, open, and cohesive against which empires are corrupt, insular, and divided.
The World Game models unequal resource distribution, trade, and the World Bank as a simplified version of global competition.
claims.temporal_scope: Classroom game example in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Trust inside the faction is built by breaking the law together.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if we cheat together, we are much more likely to trust each other
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's secret-society model its dark mechanism: shared guilt becomes social glue.

signature_moments.moment: The forecast is not who has more stuff; it is who is alive, open, and cohesive enough to exploit a corrupt center.
signature_moments.source_phrase: figure out which areas of the world are the most energetic, most open, and most cohesive
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This turns the lecture's historical model into a predictive tool.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Shared law-breaking that creates trust because all participants become punishable together.
The mythic coordination story that tells a secret society why it serves a higher god or world-ending mission.
A classroom simulation where teams with unequal resources compete to become the wealthiest country by producing commodities for a World Bank.

chronology_notes.note: This packet applies the lecture's historical model to forecasting present and future power shifts.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR transcribes 'different suicides' where context indicates different secret societies.
